In Java, perhaps using a StringBuilder would be efficient for concatenation. So start with a StringBuilder, append each character multiplied by its count.

Now, let's think about the data structures. The HashMap will take O(n) time to build, where n is the length of the string. The list of entries would be O(k), where k is the number of unique characters. Sorting that list would take O(k log k) time. Then building the string is O(n) time again. So in the worst case, k is O(n), but since k is small compared to n, the sorting step is manageable.

But for the problem, the time complexity would be O(n + k log k), which is acceptable for n up to 5*10^5.

class Solution {
    public String frequencySort(String s) {
        HashMap<Character, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
        for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
            freq.put(c, freq.getOrDefault(c, 0) + 1);
        }
        
        List<Map.Entry<Character, Integer>> entries = new ArrayList<>(freq.entrySet());
        entries.sort((a, b) -> b.getValue().compareTo(a.getValue()));
        
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        for (Map.Entry<Character, Integer> entry : entries) {
            char c = entry.getKey();
            int count = entry.getValue();
            for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) {
                sb.append(c);
            }
        }
        return sb.toString();
    }
}

Wait, but in Java, the Map.Entry's getValue() is O(1). So the code should work.

Since k is O(1) (since there are a limited number of possible characters, as per the problem statement), the overall time complexity is O(n). However, in general, if k is not a constant, the time complexity is O(n + k log k). But given the constraints, it's acceptable.

Space complexity is O(k) for the frequency map and O(k) for the list. Plus O(n) for the StringBuilder. So overall O(n) space.

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public String frequencySort(String s) {
        HashMap<Character, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
        for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
            freq.put(c, freq.getOrDefault(c, 0) + 1);
        }
        
        List<Map.Entry<Character, Integer>> entries = new ArrayList<>(freq.entrySet());
        entries.sort((a, b) -> b.getValue().compareTo(a.getValue()));
        
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        for (Map.Entry<Character, Integer> entry : entries) {
            char c = entry.getKey();
            int count = entry.getValue();
            for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) {
                sb.append(c);
            }
        }
        return sb.toString();
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a frequency map to count character occurrences, sorts the entries by descending frequency, and constructs the result string. Key strengths include efficient O(n) time for large inputs and handling all edge cases. Weaknesses are minor: sorting same-frequency characters in arbitrary order, but this is allowed by the problem.
